summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Fog Creek Software is seeking a documentary filmmaker to chronicle their summer intern program, where four competitively selected interns will build a complete software product from concept to paying customers in ten weeks. The post details the opportunity, comparing it favorably to reality TV while emphasizing it as a serious software development project. Fog Creek offers a modest $5,000 grant plus expenses but the filmmaker would own all rights to the finished film. Spolsky leverages his blog's large audience (350,000 monthly unique visitors) as a distribution channel for the eventual documentary. The post is essentially a casting call structured as a FAQ, covering financing, schedule, content ideas, and application details.
key_points: Fog Creek hired four summer interns from 800+ applicants to build a real software product from scratch in ten weeks, rather than assigning typical busywork
The documentary opportunity is framed as filmmaker-owned intellectual property with only a modest subsidy from Fog Creek, not a corporate industrial film
Spolsky positions Joel on Software's massive readership as a built-in distribution and promotion channel for the finished documentary
The project is explicitly contrasted with reality TV — serious development work, no manufactured drama or firings
The post reveals Spolsky's instinct for turning internal company activities into public-facing content and marketing
money_quotes: Instead of wasting their talents giving them the usual dull and unimportant tasks of a typical summer internship, we decided to let the interns create a complete new software product, from beginning to end, over the course of one summer.
Unlike The Apprentice, our business challenge will be a serious ten week project that becomes a real product, not four hours of wrapping chocolate bars disguised as a "business challenge."
And we don't have a crazy boss with a bad combover firing people every week.
Our selection process was extremely competitive, with over 800 kids applying for only four positions.
The goal is to tell the story of a the summer internship and the new product development simultaneously, in the form of a fairly level-headed documentary.
key_insight: Spolsky recognized that the process of building software is itself compelling content worth documenting, and used an intern project as both a genuine product development effort and a public storytelling opportunity.
